1: Chapter 5 continues Eliphaz's first speech begun in Job 4. Eliphaz is the eldest and most senior of the three friends (his later speech in Job 15 calls him the first to speak). The question 'to which of the holy ones will you appeal?' invokes the concept of the divine council — the assembly of heavenly beings who serve YHWH (1 Kings 22:19-22; Ps 82; Job 89:5-7; Isaiah 6:1-8). In Job's context the question is rhetorical: no member of the divine court will intercede for Job because (Eliphaz assumes) Job is guilty. The 'holy ones' (qĕḏōšîm) in the council appear in Zechariah 14:5; Daniel 8:13; and the Qumran community called itself the 'congregation of the holy ones.'
2: Eliphaz cites a wisdom maxim: anger (kāʿas, vexation) kills the fool and jealousy (qinʾāh, zeal/envy) destroys the credulous. This connects to the wisdom tradition's warnings about uncontrolled passion as self-destructive: Proverbs 12:16 ('a fool shows his annoyance at once'); Proverbs 14:17,29; Job 27:3-4 on the destructive power of anger and envy. The verse implies Job's complaints are self-destructive expressions of a fool's anger — a pointed personal accusation wrapped in the form of a general proverb.
3: Eliphaz appeals to his personal observation as epistemological authority — 'I have seen' (rāʾîṯî) — a standard wisdom source alongside tradition (v27: 'we have examined') and dream-vision (Job 4:12-17). The retributive framework is confirmed by the sudden cursing (ʾārar) of the fool's household; the suddenness (pitʾōm) is characteristic of the wisdom tradition's expectation of divine justice (Proverbs 6:15; Job 29:1). The curse on offspring for the father's sin reflects the Deuteronomic understanding of multigenerational consequences (Exodus 20:5; Deuteronomy 5:9).
4: The crushing of the fool's children 'at the city gate' (šaʿar) places the justice scenario in the legal-commercial hub of the ancient city. The city gate was where legal cases were heard (Ruth 4:1-11; Amos 5:12,15; Proverbs 31:23), disputes adjudicated, and commerce conducted. To be crushed at the gate without a rescuer (mōšîaʿ) is to lack all the protective networks that city-gate society provided — legal advocates, family connections, or divine intervention.
5: The image of the hungry eating up the fool's harvest 'from among the thorns' (min-qōṣ) reflects the agricultural vulnerability of the ancient Near East: inadequate fencing, drought, and raider attacks could devastate a harvest. The verse may preserve a proverb about the fragility of ill-gotten or unprotected gains. The motif of others consuming what the guilty planted is a standard covenant-curse formula (Deuteronomy 28:30,33: 'you will plant a vineyard but not enjoy it'; Leviticus 26:16).
6: Verse 6-7 form a two-part unit: v6 states the negative (trouble does not arise spontaneously from nature), v7 states the positive counterpart. The rhetorical structure mirrors ANE wisdom-literature forms where negation precedes affirmation to make the affirmation stronger. The verse denies that suffering is a natural accident — instead it is purposefully connected to human behavior, setting up Eliphaz's disciplinary theology in v17.
7: The phrase 'sons of Resheph fly upward' (bĕnê-rešep̄ yagbîhû ʿûp̄) preserves a reference to Resheph, the Canaanite god of plague, pestilence, and burning fever (attested in Ugaritic texts as rsp and in Egyptian inscriptions where Resheph is depicted as an archer). The 'sons of Resheph' are sparks or arrows — the fiery symptoms of plague. The MKT renders it as 'sparks fly upward' following the common English interpretation, but the mythological background suggests that trouble in human life has the inevitability of a deity's arrows being released. The image is demythologized compared to its Canaanite origin but retains the ANE conception of suffering as divinely launched projectiles (see also 6:4 where Job uses 'arrows of Shaddai').
8: Eliphaz's advice is framed in first-person hypothetical: 'as for me, I would seek God' (ʾănî ʾeḏrōš ʾel-ʾēl). The seeking-God vocabulary is central to Israel's worship tradition (Psalms 14:2; Job 27:8; Isaiah 55:6; Amos 5:4-6 'seek me and live'; the repeated 'seek YHWH/God' formula in the prophets and Psalms). Eliphaz is recommending exactly what the wisdom tradition demands — but in a context where Job is already seeking and not finding, the advice becomes inadvertently ironic.
9: The praise of God as doing 'great and unsearchable things' (gĕḏōlôṯ wĕʾên ḥēqer) echoes a standard doxological formula found in the Psalms (Job 77:14; Job 86:10; Job 136:4) and Isaiah (Job 40:28 'his understanding is unsearchable'). The participial style of vv9-16 — listing God's mighty acts — is a hymnic form characteristic of the Psalms (e.g., Psalms 103:3-10; 104; 107) and wisdom poetry; the friends frequently employ the hymn-form as part of their arguments, using praise of divine power as implicit leverage against Job's complaint.
10: Rain as divine blessing is a constant OT theological claim: Deuteronomy 11:11-14 makes rain in its season the specific covenant-blessing contingent on obedience; Psalms 65:9-13 celebrates God watering the earth; Isaiah 55:10 uses rain as a metaphor for the word of God accomplishing its purpose. In the ANE context of semi-arid Palestine and Mesopotamia, where rain determined survival, its attribution to YHWH rather than Baal (the storm/rain deity; 1 Kgs 18) was a theological claim about where true power resided.
11: The lifting-of-the-lowly and raising-of-mourners formula appears throughout Israel's worship tradition as a foundational attribute of YHWH's justice: 1 Samuel 2:7-8 (Hannah's Song: 'he raises the poor from the dust'); Psalms 113:7-9; Luke 1:52 (Magnificat). The pairing of 'low ones' (šĕp̄ālîm) with those in mourning (qĕḏārîm, 'darkened/in mourning-dress') reflects the double vulnerability of poverty and grief that divine justice specifically addresses.
12: The frustration of the cunning's schemes is a wisdom-tradition theme developed in Proverbs 3:7 ('do not be wise in your own eyes'); Job 11:2 ('pride comes, then disgrace'); and Job 26:27 ('whoever digs a pit will fall in it'). In the prophets, the cunning who devise evil against the helpless are specifically targeted by divine reversal (Isaiah 29:14; Micah 2:1-3). The wisdom tradition consistently argues that elaborate human scheming fails because it does not account for divine oversight.
13: This verse is directly quoted by Paul in 1 Corinthians 3:19 ('he catches the wise in their craftiness' — the only direct NT quotation from the book of Job). Paul deploys it in his argument that worldly wisdom is foolishness before God and that the Corinthians should not boast in human leaders. The NT application shifts the context: Eliphaz uses it as a warning to Job, but Paul uses it to undercut the very kind of confident wisdom-claiming that Eliphaz represents. Eliphaz quotes wisdom to trap Job; Paul quotes Job to unmask the trap of wisdom-confidence itself.
14: The reversal of light and darkness — 'they encounter darkness in broad daylight' — is a standard prophetic sign of divine judgment against the corrupt powerful (Amos 5:18-20: the Day of YHWH as darkness, not light; Isaiah 8:22; Job 59:9-10: 'we grope like the blind along the wall... we stumble at noon as in the twilight'). The midday darkness as punishment is also the plague-of-darkness motif from Exodus 10:21-23, where Egypt experiences darkness while Israel has light — the same reversal Eliphaz describes.
15: The salvation of the poor from the 'cutting words' (ḥereb, literally 'sword') of enemies reflects the ancient understanding that verbal violence — false accusation, slander, and incitement — was a weapon comparable to physical harm. The Psalms repeatedly describe the tongue as a sword (Job 57:4; Job 64:3; Proverbs 12:18); in the legal context, false testimony could literally destroy a person's life and livelihood. 'The grip of the powerful' (min-yāḏ ḥāzāq) — the powerful hand as instrument of oppression is a standard OT justice-concern (Psalms 35:10; Job 140:4).
16: 'The poor find hope' (wattihî lĕʾeḇyôn tiqwāh) — tiqwāh (hope) as the specific possession of those the world has written off is a major prophetic theme: Isaiah 40:31 ('those who hope in YHWH will renew their strength'); Jeremiah 29:11 ('plans for a future and a hope'); Lamentations 3:24 ('YHWH is my portion; therefore I will hope in him'). The verse ends 'injustice is silenced' (ʿawlāh qāp̄ĕṣāh pîhā = injustice shuts its mouth) — the personification of injustice as a speaking mouth that gets silenced when God acts is a vivid legal metaphor.
17: 'Blessed is the person whom God reproves' — this beatitude formula (ʾašrê hāgāḇer ʾăšer yôḵiḥennû ʾĕlôah) is the OT antecedent to Hebrews 12:5-6, which quotes Proverbs 3:11-12 ('do not despise the LORD's discipline') in the context of suffering-as-training. The makarism/beatitude form (ʾašrê = blessed is the one who...) is characteristic of Psalm openings (Psalms 1:1; Job 32:1-2; Job 119:1-2) and the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:3-12). Eliphaz is offering Job a theological framework for his suffering — discipline leads to restoration — that is genuinely true as a general principle but falsely applied to Job's specific situation.
18: The 'wound and bandage' theology of v18 parallels Hosea 6:1 ('he has torn us but will heal us; he has struck us down and will bind up our wounds') and Isaiah 30:26 ('when YHWH binds up the bruises of his people'). The medical imagery of divine wounding-then-healing is part of the Deuteronomic and prophetic understanding of suffering as remedial: the same God who strikes also heals. Deuteronomy 32:39 makes the claim most starkly: 'I kill and I make alive; I have wounded and I will heal.' The NT fulfillment is the crucified-and-risen Christ as the wounded healer.
19: The 'six troubles... seven' formula is a graded numerical saying characteristic of Semitic wisdom literature (cf. Proverbs 6:16 'six things the LORD hates, seven that are detestable'; Amos 1:3,6,9 'for three transgressions... and for four'). The formula expresses totality and completeness — no matter how many troubles come (up to the symbolic limit of seven), God will deliver. The numerical formula appears in Ugaritic literature as well, confirming its ANE wisdom-genre roots. The promise of complete deliverance through all combinations of trouble is meant to reassure Job, though it ironically describes exactly the comprehensive suffering Job has already experienced.
20: Redemption from famine and war are the two paradigmatic threats to ancient Israelite life: famine controlled by rain and harvest (connected to Deut 11 obedience-rain theology), and war as the primary external threat. The promise to redeem from 'the lethal edge of the sword' (mîḏê ḥāreḇ) uses the covenant-protection vocabulary of Psalms 144:10 and the Deuteronomic blessing-curses, where military defeat is a specific covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:25-26) and military victory a covenant blessing.
21: The 'lash of the tongue' (šôṭ lāšôn) — the tongue as a whip is a powerful wisdom metaphor for destructive speech; the same metaphor appears in Proverbs 18:21 ('death and life are in the power of the tongue'); Psalms 31:20 ('from the strife of tongues'); and the NT: James 3:5-8 (the tongue as a fire and unruly evil). The promise to be sheltered from verbal attack is particularly poignant given that Job's suffering has included the verbal attacks of his three friends and the mockery of v9.
22: 'You will laugh at destruction and famine' — śāḥaq (to laugh) directed at the threats of destruction (šôḏ) and famine (kāp̄ān) is the confidence of the protected covenant member. The same laughter-at-enemies motif appears in Psalms 2:4 (God laughing at the nations), Psalms 37:13 (YHWH laughing at the wicked), and the wisdom theme that the righteous will outlive all threats (Proverbs 1:26). 'Wild animals of the land' (ḥayyôṯ hāʾāreṣ) — in the ancient Near East, predatory animals were a genuine threat to human habitation; they feature in Deuteronomy 28:26 as a covenant curse.
23: 'A covenant with the stones of the field' — the covenant (bĕrît) with inanimate natural forces is an unusual formulation that reflects the ANE understanding of the natural world as covenantally ordered. Hosea 2:18 (Israel's restoration) describes a covenant with animals and weapons: 'I will make a covenant for them with the wild animals, the birds, and the creatures that move along the ground.' The broader concept is of a creation covenant in which the righteous live in harmony with the natural order — the Edenic condition of Gen 1-2 restored. Wild animals at peace with the human reflects the 'peaceable kingdom' motif of Isaiah 11:6-9.
24: The secure home (šālôm) and full flocks are the specific components of ANE prosperity: a household (ʾōhel/nāweh) that does not experience loss, and livestock that reproduce without loss (peqûddāh = musters/counts of the flock). In the ancient pastoral economy, flock numbers were the primary indicator of wealth. The promise repeats the content of Job's pre-disaster condition as described in Job 1:3: 'seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels...' — Eliphaz is essentially promising Job his Job 1 life back if he submits.
25: Numerous offspring like the grass of the earth (keʿēśeḇ hāʾāreṣ) echoes the Abrahamic covenant-promise: Genesis 22:17 ('I will make your offspring as numerous as the stars of the sky and the sand on the seashore'); Genesis 28:14 ('your offspring will be like the dust of the earth'). The grass-of-earth simile specifically appears in Psalms 72:16 for the flourishing of the righteous king's subjects. The patriarchal promise of numerous descendants as the sign of divine blessing is here presented as the reward awaiting Job's repentance.
26: 'Come to your grave in ripe old age, like a sheaf of grain harvested in its season' — the death-in-old-age-in-its-proper-time is a standard covenant-blessing formula. Genesis 15:15 promises Abram: 'you shall go to your fathers in peace; you shall be buried in a good old age.' The harvest-sheaf metaphor for a timely, dignified death (bĕkaleah qāmeh) emphasizes the season-appropriate nature of the righteous death: not premature, not violent, not protracted, but complete and gathered at the proper moment. The agricultural metaphor integrates human life and death into the rhythms of creation's ordered bounty.
27: Eliphaz closes his speech with the claim of collective empirical validation: 'we have examined this carefully; it is true' (hinnēh-zōʾṯ ḥăqarnûhāh kēn-hîʾ). The verb ḥāqar (to search out/investigate) is the language of wisdom research (Job 8:8; Ecclesiastes 1:13; Proverbs 25:2). 'Hear it and know it for yourself' (šĕmāʿennāh wĕʾattāh ḏāʿ-lāḵ) — the imperatives of hearing and knowing frame his conclusion as a universally accessible truth. The corporate 'we' validates the three-friends' unanimity. Job's response will reject both the content and the epistemological confidence of this claim.
